{"value": "The two tables were completely different in the experience of using them. The first table was actually formatted in a way that my screen reader recognised as a table, which allowed me to navigate and compare between options very well. The actual information of green ticks and red crosses was not visible, so overall the table was useless to me with my screen reader. The second table was not formatted as a table from my screen reader's perspective; it was just a list of text. This was accessible in that I could read the particular thing being compared and whether or not it was included or not, the red or green cross or tick. So it was more useful; however, had it also been formatted correctly, it would make it much easier to compare between the options, rather than having to remember what the previous option was. The formatting of table one with the accessibility and readability of table two would be great. "}

{"value": "Yes, the accordion and date pickers were both accessible and worked relatively well. Some of the other navigation in this section was not as clear. There was one instance where I had to click a heading to make a selection, and another instance where there was an actual select button to make the selection, which was more intuitive. The whole process was not that intuitive, and the navigation was not consistent. "}

{"value": "The website is easy to navigate and not tirng to read. I would like to see the amount of scrolling reduced without reducing the font size. Perhaps the third of a page photos could be made smaller? How does it work on other devices e.g. phones?

By insisting people phone up you are either pushing them to buy the more expensive cover or look elsewhere or accidentally discriminating against people with hearing loss or anxiety who may not want to phone a noisy, busy call centre and endure hold music.
